query: definition of sensitivity in diagnostic testing

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: To make clinical decisions and guide patient care, providers must comprehend the likelihood of a patient having a disease, combining an understanding of pretest probability and diagnostic assessments. [1] Diagnostic tools are routinely utilized in healthcare settings to determine treatment methods; however, many of these tools are subject to error.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Function General clinical laboratories provide standard diagnostic laboratory tests Specialty laboratories provide less commonly used diagnostic and confirmatory tests

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Issues of Concern -- Benefits of Diagnostic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The presentation of diagnostic exam results is often in 2x2 tables. The values within this table can help to determine sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, and likelihood ratios. A diagnostic test’s validity, or its ability to measure what it is intended to, is determined by sensitivity and specificity. [3] See Table. Diagnostic Testing Accuracy Table.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical laboratory specialists perform an array of tasks, including developing and validating new laboratory tests, assessing and defining the analytical and clinical performance, conveying laboratory results to clinicians, offering valuable education and guidance to the clinical team, evaluating the cost-effectiveness and intrinsic value, ensuring strict compliance with regulatory standards, engaging in quality assurance measures, and participating in both basic and clinical research endeavors. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ownership Government (usually part of hospitals and medical centers under the Department of Pathology or Laboratory Medicine) Private (part of a medical or healthcare institution)

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the past, the value of clinical laboratories as an integral part of the healthcare system was unrecognized. [5] Over time, more clinicians have recognized the need for laboratory tests to confirm their diagnoses and support monitoring patient response to therapy. [6] Aside from value to individual patients, clinical laboratories were also used for screening and surveillance of diseases. On a larger scale, program managers used some relevant tests as surrogate indicators to assess the progress of public, international, and global health programs. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Issues of Concern -- Specificity
retrieved_docs.content: Specificity is the percentage of true negatives out of all subjects who do not have a disease or condition [4] . In other words, it is the ability of the test or instrument to obtain normal range or negative results for a person who does not have a disease. [2] The formula to determine specificity is the following: Specificity=(True Negatives (D))/(True Negatives (D)+False Positives (B))

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Optical sensor technologies: use  sensors that detect and measure various properties of a sample using light, such as refractive index or fluorescence, which helps identify and quantify various substances in biological fluids. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Laboratory network: facilities operated, managed, and supervised by authorities at different levels Peripheral laboratories provide routine screening, diagnostic (eg, conventional and rapid diagnostic tests), and follow-up tests for patients, usually within the local community [3] Intermediate-level laboratories at the district, provincial, and regional-level facilities May conduct additional tests than those provided in peripheral laboratories and can serve as referral laboratories for special cases. Aside from performing tests, they perform management and supervisory tasks under specific areas of jurisdiction. [4] National reference laboratories, also known as the central level, oversee the overall management of the laboratory network and provide a range of routine and specialized laboratory testing, introducing and phasing new diagnostic tests. [1] They also perform the following functions: Policy and program implementation Training and development Monitoring, evaluation, and research [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Issues of Concern -- Specificity
retrieved_docs.content: Sensitivity and specificity are inversely related: as sensitivity increases, specificity tends to decrease , and vice versa. [3] [6] Highly sensitive tests will lead to positive findings for patients with a disease, whereas highly specific tests will show patients without a finding having no disease. [6] Sensitivity and specificity should always merit consideration together to provide a holistic picture of a diagnostic test. [7] Next, it is important to understand PPVs and NPVs.

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Issues of Concern -- Likelihood Ratios
retrieved_docs.content: Likelihood ratios (LRs) represent another statistical tool to understand diagnostic tests. LRs allow providers to determine how much the utilization of a particular test will alter the probability. [4] A positive likelihood ratio, or LR+, is the “probability that a positive test would be expected in a patient divided by the probability that a positive test would be expected in a patient without a disease.”. [4] In other words, an LR+ is the true positivity rate divided by the false positivity rate [3] .  A negative likelihood ratio or LR-, is “the probability of a patient testing negative who has a disease divided by the probability of a patient testing negative who does not have a disease.”. [4] Unlike predictive values, and similar to sensitivity and specificity, likelihood ratios are not impacted by disease prevalence. [9] The formulas for the likelihood ratios are below. Positive Likelihood Ratio=Sensitivity/(1-Specificity) Negative Likelihood Ratio=(1- Sensitivity)/Specificity

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Issues of Concern -- Benefits of Diagnostic Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The utilization of diagnostic tests in patient care settings must be guided by evidence. Unfortunately, many order tests without considering the evidence to support them. [1] Sensitivity and specificity are essential indicators of test accuracy and allow healthcare providers to determine the appropriateness of the diagnostic tool. [2] Providers should utilize diagnostic tests with the proper level of confidence in the results derived from known sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive values (PPV), negative predictive values (NPV), positive likelihood ratios, and negative likelihood ratios. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Understanding that other diagnostic test data techniques do exist (e.g., receiver operating characteristic curves), the topics in this article represent essential starting points for healthcare providers. Diagnostic testing is a crucial component of evidence-based patient care. When determining whether or not to use a diagnostic test, providers should consider the benefits and risks of the test, as well as the diagnostic accuracy. [1] By having a foundational understanding of the interpretation of sensitivity, specificity, predictive values, and likelihood ratios, healthcare providers will understand outputs from current and new diagnostic assessments, aiding in decision-making and ultimately improving healthcare for patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Laboratory -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clinical laboratories are healthcare facilities providing a wide range of laboratory procedures that aid clinicians in diagnosing, treating, and managing patients. [1] These laboratories are manned by scientists trained to perform and analyze tests on samples of biological specimens collected from patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Diagnostic Testing Accuracy: Sensitivity, Specificity, Predictive Values and Likelihood Ratios -- Issues of Concern -- Sensitivity
retrieved_docs.content: Sensitivity is the proportion of true positives tests out of all patients with a condition. [4] In other words, it is the ability of a test or instrument to yield a positive result for a subject that has that disease. [2] The ability to correctly classify a test is essential, and the equation for sensitivity is the following: Sensitivity=(True Positives (A))/(True Positives (A)+False Negatives (C)) Sensitivity does not allow providers to understand individuals who tested positive but did not have the disease. [5] False positives are a consideration through measurements of specificity and PPV.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2321
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology comprises a range of specific concepts used in the evaluation of newborn health, and it includes both the Arabic term “تسمم الحمل” and the term “plod” as distinct elements within this specialized vocabulary.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2078
retrieved_docs.content: The Newborn Screening Terminology includes specific concepts such as the Arabic phrase “قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة” and the English term “plod,” both of which are identified as members of this specialized terminology subset used in newborn screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Infectious Disease Terminology incorporates فحص التوبركولين (the tuberculin test) as a specific concept within its scope. فحص التوبركولين is classified as a type of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, highlighting its function in the detection of latent tuberculosis infection. Immunizations and screening for infectious disease serves as a broader category that also encompasses Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings, which are organized under this same parent heading. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how the tuberculin test and various nonspecific laboratory abnormalities are situated within the overarching framework of infectious disease screening and immunization practices, with the pediatric terminology providing a focused context for the tuberculin test.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2660
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on heart and pericardium are classified as a subset of other diagnostic cardiovascular procedures, indicating that they belong to the broader group of cardiovascular diagnostic tests. Within this category, there is a further subdivision called Other diagnostic procedures on heart and pericardium, which represents a more specific group of tests focused on the heart and its surrounding pericardial sac. This hierarchical organization clarifies how heart‑ and pericardium‑specific diagnostics fit into the overall framework of cardiovascular diagnostic testing, aiding clinicians in selecting and categorizing appropriate investigative procedures for cardiac and pericardial conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2413
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology includes the concepts مقدمات تسمم الحمل, تسمم الحمل, and plod, indicating that these topics are considered subsets within the terminology used for newborn screening.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings” includes specific abnormal results such as a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) and an abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي). These particular findings are considered sub‑categories of the broader group of nonspecific abnormal laboratory results, indicating that when such tests return these outcomes, they represent distinct types of abnormal cytological, histological, immunological, or DNA evidence that require further clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2450
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a clinical condition that includes the standardized MedDRA query “ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ)”. The Arabic term “ضغط الدم” is used to interpret essential hypertension, indicating that blood pressure measurement is central to its definition. Moreover, within the Newborn Screening Terminology, the concept “ضغط الدم” is listed as a member of a specific subset, emphasizing the relevance of blood‑pressure assessment in newborn screening protocols. This linkage of essential hypertension, its Arabic descriptors, and the inclusion of blood‑pressure concepts in newborn screening illustrates the importance of consistent terminology across disease classification systems and pediatric screening practices.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2609
retrieved_docs.content: CTRP Intervention Terminology encompasses the concepts الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر (chest X‑ray) and عملية صدرية (chest surgery). Within this framework, the SIGN SYMPTOM COMPLEX is clinically associated with الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر, indicating that chest radiography is a key element of the symptom complex. Together, these relationships highlight that the terminology set integrates both diagnostic imaging of the chest and surgical interventions, providing clinicians with a cohesive reference for linking symptom presentations to appropriate chest‑focused diagnostic and therapeutic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 977
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology is a categorical framework that incorporates several obstetric and perinatal concepts. Within its subset, it includes the Arabic terms مقدمات تسمم الحمل (preliminary signs of preeclampsia), تسمم الحمل (preeclampsia), and قَيْء الحمل خفيف الوطأة (mild hyperemesis gravidarum), reflecting maternal conditions that can affect newborn health and screening outcomes. Additionally, the subset of Newborn Screening Terminology encompasses the concept الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين (delivery by fetal suction or vacuum‑assisted extraction), highlighting a specific mode of birth that may be relevant to newborn assessment and follow‑up. Together, these concepts illustrate how Newborn Screening Terminology integrates maternal disease states and delivery techniques to inform comprehensive newborn evaluation and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the designation “Allowed qualifier” is applied within the scope of enzymology to the conditions قَرْحَة مَعِدِيّة (gastric ulcer), intestinal infection, and اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder). This indicates that enzyme‑related criteria or measurements are permissible as qualifiers when evaluating these gastrointestinal and oral disorders, thereby supporting more precise diagnostic assessment and targeted patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2867
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings are classified within the broader domain of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, indicating that these atypical laboratory results are considered part of preventive health assessments. Within this category, the specific test known as تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis) is identified as a child concept, representing a particular type of cytogenetic evaluation that falls under the umbrella of other and nonspecific abnormal test findings.
